narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, we know how much effort and determination it takes to pursue this path, and we're here to help you understand the admission decision in a clear way. Your application included your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, an LSAT score of 28, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 2, middle family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time. The university uses a predictive model that estimates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it forecasted a 94% chance that you would not pass, leading to the rejection of your application. This high predicted risk means the model saw your profile as more likely to face challenges on the bar compared to those approved. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 2, well below the typical student's 5.53 average, signaling to the model weaker performance in later undergrad years. Next in importance was your LSAT score of 28, significantly lower than the average of 36.7, which strongly nudged the prediction toward failure. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also below the average of 5.71, added to this concern by highlighting early academic struggles. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, close to the average of 3.21, actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction slightly toward passing success. Your middle family income and full-time enrollment plans had smaller influences, with income mildly contributing to the risk but full-time status offering a tiny boost. Overall, your academic rankings and LSAT stood out as the biggest hurdles compared to typical admitted students, who generally show stronger deciles and test scores. While this is disappointing, especially given the barriers many Black women like yourself overcome in higher education, it's not the end of your journey. We recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and reapplying next cycle with these improvements to shift your predicted odds. You're capable of great things—keep pushing forward.
